title: Electric Power Steering (EPS) Structure Description - EPS Control

blocks.text: Electric Power Steering (EPS) Structure Description - EPS Control
The base current is the basic current value to drive the motor. The base current is calculated by the steering torque and vehicle speed.

Due to the inertia of the rotor, motors tend to lack torque when starting and continue torque when stopping. Inertia correction reduces the effect of inertia by increasing the base current during speed increases and decreasing it during deceleration. Inertia correction is performed by steering torque, vehicle speed and motor speed.

During braking and cornering, the steering wheel may experience small vibrations from the road surface. Damping compensation reduces these small vibrations of the steering wheel by providing a dampening effect through motor current control. Damping compensation is performed based on steering torque, vehicle speed, and motor speed.

The target current is the target current value for feedback control of the motor. The target current is determined by applying inertia and damping corrections to the base current and adding the steering torque direction.
Current feedback control monitors the motor current with a sensor to reduce the deviation of the motor current from the target current and drive the motor with high accuracy.
The unloader control protects the system by reducing the motor current when the steering wheel is end held. The control is performed by steering torque and motor speed.

The motor drive circuit consists of a computer, FET bridge drive circuit, H-bridge circuit, power relay, fail-safe relay, motor current detection circuit, and motor. The computer calculates the appropriate current and rotates the motor by controlling the duty cycle of the FET drive circuit.

The motor output limit control protects the system by decreasing the motor current when large steering wheel forces are repeatedly applied, such as in continuous deferral. This control causes a gradual decrease in power assist force. The return of power assist force begins gradually at a steering torque of 0 N-m (0 kgf-m) or when the ignition switch is turned off, and it may take up to 17.3 minutes for the system to return to normal assist force.
